title: Goldman Sachs CEO backs Clarity Act despite banking industry's concerns over stablecoin rules
dek: David Solomon, CEO of Goldman Sachs, publicly endorsed the Clarity Act on Thursday, saying it creates a level playing field and market stability, even while acknowledging the bill is not perfect. His support breaks from JPMorgan Chase and other major banks, which argue the bill's stablecoin yield provisions unfairly disadvantage traditional finance by allowing crypto firms to offer yield-bearing products without bank-level regulatory oversight.
author: Crypto Cronkite
key_fact: Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon stated he is very supportive of moving the Clarity Act forward to establish market structure and allow innovation, directly contradicting JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon's warning that banks will not accept stablecoin yield provisions and the bill will eventually blow up if they stand.
bottom_line: Watch whether other major banks follow JPMorgan's opposition or Goldman's support as the Clarity Act approaches a Senate floor vote. The bill's passage likely hinges on whether lawmakers address the stablecoin yield concern in ways that preserve the security model crypto platforms are pushing for while satisfying banking sector demands for parity. A failure to reconcile those positions could stall the legislation even with institutional buy-in from parts of Wall Street.
body: Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon broke ranks with JPMorgan Chase and other major banks on Thursday to publicly endorse the Clarity Act, a comprehensive digital asset regulatory bill facing a Senate floor vote next week, according to CoinDesk's reporting.

Solomon acknowledged the legislation is "not perfect," but argued it serves a critical function: establishing clear market structure and enabling innovation. "The CLARITY Act, like all legislation, is not perfect. And there are lots of things that you could debate and argue about," Solomon stated. "But I think one of the most important things that it does is that it creates a level playing field to enhance market stability and allow these markets to develop appropriately. I'm very supportive of moving the CLARITY Act forward, so we can get some market structure in place and start to move the innovation process along."

His position stands in direct opposition to JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon, who has argued the bill's stablecoin provisions create a competitive disadvantage for traditional banks. The central tension: the Clarity Act would allow crypto companies to offer yield-bearing stablecoin products, which function like bank deposits but remain outside the regulatory and capital framework imposed on traditional financial institutions. Dimon told Fox Business in May that this structure "allows them to effectively pay interest on deposits, stablecoins or something like that, without protection that they should have." He added, "The banks will not accept it that way. I'm not worried about stablecoins but if it happened I'm telling you I will have nothing to do with it and it will eventually blow up." JPMorgan published a June blog post arguing that firms offering products functioning like traditional bank accounts should face comparable oversight and consumer protections, per CoinDesk's reporting.

The stablecoin yield dispute has become one of the biggest sticking points in Clarity Act negotiations, according to CoinDesk's reporting. Banking executives broadly contend that crypto firms offering bank-like products should be regulated comparably to banks. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong has countered that banks are lobbying lawmakers to restrict stablecoin rewards because they threaten banks' deposit-based business models, per CoinDesk's reporting.

The Clarity Act would establish a regulatory framework for digital assets by defining the respective roles of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, according to CoinDesk. Republican senators circulated updated text ahead of the possible Senate floor vote, and lawmakers are still negotiating provisions covering stablecoin issuers, consumer protections and yield-bearing products before the bill can advance through Congress.

Solomon has previously signaled openness to thoughtful crypto regulation. In February, he stated: "When you burden this system with excessive regulation, you start to extract capital. But regulation has got to be done thoughtfully, and we've got to get it right."

The split between Goldman and JPMorgan reflects a broader institutional divide: some Wall Street firms see regulated crypto markets as a business opportunity and market evolution; others view stablecoin features as regulatory arbitrage that threatens their core deposit franchises. Goldman's public backing strengthens the bill's institutional support even as banking-industry opposition remains a material headwind.
